Question: What is sugar disease?
Answer: Sugar disease is another name for diabetes. It's a condition where your body has trouble controlling blood sugar levels. When you eat food, your body turns it into sugar (glucose) for energy, but with diabetes, this sugar stays in your blood instead of going into your cells.

Question: My sugar is high what to do?
Answer: If your sugar is high: 1) Drink plenty of water to help flush it out. 2) Go for a 15-30 minute walk if you feel okay. 3) Don't eat sugary foods or drinks. 4) Take your medicine if the doctor gave you any. 5) If sugar stays very high (over 300) or you feel sick, see a doctor right away.

Question: My sugar is low what to do?
Answer: If your sugar is low: Eat or drink something with sugar quickly - like fruit juice, candy, or glucose tablets. Wait 15 minutes and check again. If still low, eat more sugar. Once you feel better, eat a proper meal with protein. If you faint or can't swallow, someone should call for emergency help.

Question: How do I know if I have sugar problem?
Answer: Signs of sugar problem (diabetes): You feel very thirsty all the time, need to pee a lot especially at night, feel very tired, lose weight without trying, your cuts don't heal fast, your vision gets blurry, and you feel hungry even after eating. See a doctor for a blood test to confirm.

Question: What sugar level is normal?
Answer: Normal sugar levels: Before eating (fasting) should be 70-99 mg/dL. After eating (2 hours) should be under 140 mg/dL. If your fasting sugar is 100-125, you have pre-diabetes. If it's 126 or higher, you have diabetes. Get tested by a doctor to know your exact levels.

Question: Can I eat rice if I have sugar?
Answer: Yes, you can eat rice but in small amounts. Choose brown rice instead of white rice - it's better for sugar control. Don't eat too much at one time (about half a cup cooked). Eat rice with vegetables and meat/fish to slow down sugar rise. Check your sugar after eating to see how your body reacts.

Question: Can I eat banana if I have sugar?
Answer: Bananas have a lot of natural sugar, so eat them carefully. It's better to choose other fruits like apples, oranges, or berries which have less sugar. If you really want banana, eat only half and pair it with some nuts or cheese. Always check your sugar after to see how it affects you.

Question: Can I eat mango with diabetes?
Answer: Mangoes are high in natural sugar, so be careful. If you want to eat mango, have only a small piece (2-3 slices), not a whole mango. Eat it as part of a meal, not alone. Better fruit choices are berries, apples, and oranges. Monitor your sugar after eating to understand your body's reaction.

Question: What should diabetic person eat?
Answer: Good foods for sugar patients: Vegetables like spinach, cabbage, beans, and tomatoes. Whole grains like brown rice and whole wheat roti. Proteins like fish, chicken, eggs, dal, and paneer. Healthy fats from nuts and seeds. Low-sugar fruits like berries and apples. Avoid white rice, white bread, sweets, and sugary drinks.

Question: What food increases sugar fast?
Answer: Foods that increase sugar quickly: White rice, white bread, maida products, sweets, candy, soft drinks, fruit juices, potatoes, sugary breakfast cereals, and anything made with white sugar. These foods should be avoided or eaten in very small amounts if you have diabetes.

Question: How to reduce sugar in body?
Answer: To reduce sugar: 1) Walk or exercise for 30 minutes daily. 2) Drink plenty of water. 3) Eat more vegetables and less rice/bread. 4) Stop eating sweets and drinking soft drinks. 5) Take your medicine regularly. 6) Reduce stress with deep breathing or prayer. 7) Get enough sleep (7-8 hours).

Question: Why does sugar increase?
Answer: Sugar can increase because of: eating too much rice, bread, or sweets; not exercising; missing your medicine; being stressed or worried; not sleeping well; being sick with fever or infection; and sometimes for no clear reason. Keep a food diary to find out what makes YOUR sugar go up.

Question: Is diabetes serious?
Answer: Yes, diabetes is serious if not controlled. High sugar over time can damage your eyes (blindness), kidneys (kidney failure), heart (heart attack), nerves (numbness in feet), and cause wounds that don't heal. But with proper diet, exercise, and medicine, you can live a normal healthy life.

Question: Can sugar be cured?
Answer: Type 2 diabetes (the common type) cannot be fully cured, but it can be controlled or even reversed with weight loss and healthy lifestyle. Some people who lose a lot of weight and eat healthy no longer need medicine. Type 1 diabetes cannot be cured and needs insulin for life.

Question: How does walking help sugar?
Answer: Walking helps sugar by: making your body use sugar for energy (lowers blood sugar), making insulin work better in your body, helping you lose weight, reducing stress, and keeping your heart healthy. Walk for 30 minutes daily, especially after meals. Even a 15-minute walk after eating helps.

Question: How much water should diabetic drink?
Answer: Diabetics should drink 8-10 glasses of water daily (about 2-3 liters). Water helps flush out extra sugar through urine, keeps you hydrated, and helps your kidneys work properly. Don't replace water with juice or soft drinks. Plain water, unsweetened tea, or lemon water are best choices.

Question: Can I eat sweets sometimes?
Answer: Having sweets occasionally is okay, but plan for it: eat a small portion only, skip other carbs (rice/bread) in that meal, take a walk after eating, and check your sugar later. Save sweets for special occasions only, not daily. Sugar-free sweets are a better option if available.

Question: What medicine is used for sugar?
Answer: Common sugar medicines: Metformin (most common, helps body use insulin better), Glimepiride and Glipizide (help pancreas make more insulin), and Insulin injections (for those who need it). Your doctor will decide which medicine is best for you based on your sugar levels and health.

Question: Can diabetes happen in children?
Answer: Yes, diabetes can happen in children. Type 1 diabetes (where body can't make insulin) usually starts in childhood. Type 2 diabetes (lifestyle-related) is also increasing in children due to unhealthy food and less exercise. If your child is always thirsty, peeing a lot, or losing weight, get them tested.

Question: Does sugar run in families?
Answer: Yes, diabetes can run in families. If your parents or siblings have diabetes, you have higher chance of getting it. But it's not 100% - lifestyle matters more. Even with family history, you can prevent or delay diabetes by maintaining healthy weight, eating well, and exercising regularly.

Question: What is insulin?
Answer: Insulin is a hormone (a chemical messenger) made by your pancreas that helps sugar from food enter your cells for energy. Without insulin, sugar stays in your blood making levels high. Type 1 diabetics can't make insulin. Type 2 diabetics either don't make enough or their body doesn't use it well.

Question: Do I need insulin?
Answer: Not everyone with diabetes needs insulin. Most Type 2 diabetics control sugar with diet, exercise, and tablets. Insulin is needed if: you have Type 1 diabetes, tablets aren't working well enough, your sugar is very high, you're pregnant, or you have kidney problems. Your doctor will decide.

Question: How often to check sugar?
Answer: How often to check depends on your situation: Type 1 diabetics check 4-10 times daily. Type 2 on tablets may check once or twice daily or few times a week. Always check when you feel unwell, before driving, and when trying new foods. Ask your doctor what's right for you.

Question: Can stress increase sugar?
Answer: Yes, stress definitely increases sugar. When you're stressed, your body releases hormones that raise blood sugar. Worrying, family problems, work pressure, and lack of sleep all affect sugar. To manage: practice deep breathing, take walks, pray or meditate, talk to family, and get enough sleep.

Question: Can I work with diabetes?
Answer: Yes, you can work normally with diabetes. Many successful people have diabetes. Just manage it well: take medicine on time, eat regular meals, keep snacks handy for low sugar, stay hydrated, take breaks to walk, inform a trusted colleague about your condition, and attend regular doctor checkups.

Question: What happens if I don't control sugar?
Answer: If sugar stays high for a long time, it damages your body slowly: eyes can go blind (retinopathy), kidneys can fail (nephropathy), feet can get numb and wounds won't heal (neuropathy), heart disease and stroke risk increases, and healing from any illness becomes difficult. Control now to stay healthy later.

Question: Can diabetics fast?
Answer: Fasting can be risky for diabetics. If you want to fast for religious reasons, talk to your doctor first. Medicine doses may need to change. Break fast immediately if sugar goes too low (below 70) or too high (above 300), or if you feel unwell. Keep glucose tablets ready.

Question: What is pre-diabetes?
Answer: Pre-diabetes means your sugar is higher than normal but not high enough to be diabetes yet. Fasting sugar 100-125 mg/dL is pre-diabetes. This is a warning sign - with diet changes and exercise, you can often prevent full diabetes. Get tested and take action now.

Question: How to prevent diabetes?
Answer: Prevent diabetes by: maintaining healthy weight (lose weight if overweight), exercising 30 minutes daily, eating more vegetables and less processed food, avoiding sugary drinks, not smoking, limiting alcohol, managing stress, and getting enough sleep. If you have family history, get regular sugar tests.

Question: Can diabetics eat eggs?
Answer: Yes, eggs are good for diabetics. They are high in protein and don't raise blood sugar. You can eat 1-2 eggs daily. Boiled, poached, or scrambled eggs are better than fried. Eggs keep you full longer and provide important nutrients. They're a great breakfast choice for diabetics.

Question: Can diabetics drink milk?
Answer: Yes, diabetics can drink milk in moderation - about 1-2 cups daily. Choose low-fat or skim milk over full cream. Avoid flavored or sweetened milk. Milk has natural sugar (lactose), so count it in your daily carbs. Curd (yogurt) without added sugar is also a good option.

Question: What is sugar test called?
Answer: Sugar tests include: Fasting Blood Sugar (FBS) - done after not eating for 8 hours. Random Blood Sugar - done anytime. Post-meal sugar - done 2 hours after eating. HbA1c - shows average sugar over 3 months. Your doctor will tell you which tests you need.

Question: What does HbA1c mean?
Answer: HbA1c is a blood test that shows your average sugar control over the past 2-3 months. Normal is below 5.7%, pre-diabetes is 5.7-6.4%, diabetes is 6.5% or higher. For diabetics, target is usually below 7%. This test is done every 3 months to see how well you're managing.

Question: Why do wounds heal slowly?
Answer: High sugar slows wound healing because: it damages blood vessels so less blood reaches wounds, it weakens your immune system so infections happen easily, and it damages nerves so you may not feel injuries. Control your sugar, check your feet daily, and see doctor quickly for any wounds.

Question: Why do I pee so much?
Answer: Frequent urination in diabetes happens because: when blood sugar is high, your kidneys try to remove extra sugar through urine. This means more urine production and more bathroom trips, especially at night. As you lose water, you also feel more thirsty. Controlling sugar will reduce this problem.

Question: Why am I always thirsty?
Answer: Excessive thirst in diabetes is caused by: when sugar is high, your body tries to flush it out through urine. This makes you dehydrated. Your body signals thirst to replace lost water. Drinking more water helps, but the real solution is controlling your blood sugar levels.

Question: Can diabetes cause tiredness?
Answer: Yes, diabetes causes tiredness because: sugar in your blood can't get into cells properly, so cells don't get energy they need. High sugar also makes you pee more causing dehydration. Poor sleep from night urination adds to fatigue. Good sugar control will improve your energy.

Question: Can diabetes affect eyes?
Answer: Yes, high sugar damages tiny blood vessels in your eyes over time. This is called diabetic retinopathy. Early stages have no symptoms. Later you may see floaters, blurry vision, or dark spots. Get yearly eye exams even if vision seems fine. Early treatment prevents blindness.

Question: Can diabetes cause foot problems?
Answer: Yes, diabetes can cause serious foot problems: nerve damage makes you not feel injuries, poor blood flow means wounds don't heal, and high sugar increases infection risk. Check feet daily for cuts or blisters, wear proper shoes, don't walk barefoot, and see doctor quickly for any foot wounds.

Question: Can I live a normal life with diabetes?
Answer: Yes, absolutely! Millions of people live full, active lives with diabetes. The key is good management: take medicine regularly, eat healthy portions, stay active, check sugar levels, and see your doctor regularly. Many athletes, actors, and successful people have diabetes and live normally.

Question: What is a diabetic diet?
Answer: A diabetic diet is simply healthy eating: fill half your plate with vegetables, quarter with protein (dal, chicken, fish, eggs), quarter with whole grains. Avoid fried food, sweets, sugary drinks, and white rice/bread. Eat regular meals at same times daily. Drink water instead of juice.

Question: How often should I see doctor?
Answer: See your doctor every 3 months for sugar check and HbA1c test. Get yearly exams for: eyes, feet, kidneys, and heart. See doctor immediately if: sugar stays very high or very low, wounds don't heal, you have chest pain or breathing problems, or you feel very unwell.

Question: Can I travel with diabetes?
Answer: Yes, you can travel safely with diabetes. Plan ahead: carry extra medicine and supplies, keep snacks for low sugar, carry a doctor's note for medicines, wear a medical ID bracelet, know local emergency numbers, stay hydrated, and check sugar more often when traveling.

Question: What is sugar monitoring device?
Answer: Sugar monitoring devices help check blood sugar at home. Glucometers use a small blood drop from finger prick to show sugar level in seconds. Continuous Glucose Monitors (CGM) use a sensor under skin to track sugar all day. Ask your doctor which is best for you.

Question: Can I drink tea or coffee?
Answer: Yes, you can drink tea and coffee, but without sugar. Use sugar-free sweetener if needed. Limit to 2-3 cups daily. Green tea is especially good. Avoid sweetened specialty coffees (like cappuccino with sugar) and don't add full-cream milk. Black tea or coffee is best.

Question: Does weather affect sugar?
Answer: Yes, extreme weather can affect sugar: In hot weather, your body uses insulin differently, and dehydration from sweating can raise sugar. In cold weather, you may be less active and eat more, raising sugar. Check sugar more often during extreme weather and stay hydrated.

Question: Can diabetics have surgery?
Answer: Yes, diabetics can have surgery safely, but need extra care. Tell your surgeon about diabetes. Sugar will be monitored closely before, during, and after surgery. You may need to adjust medicine or insulin. Good sugar control helps wounds heal faster after surgery.

Question: What fruits can diabetics eat?
Answer: Best fruits for diabetics: Berries (strawberry, blueberry), apples, oranges, guava, papaya (small portion), pears, and watermelon (small portion). Eat fresh whole fruit, not juice. Eat fruit as snack or with meal, not on empty stomach. Avoid or limit: mangoes, bananas, grapes, and chickoo.
